1: shalach lachmekha al penei hamayim: 'Cast your bread upon the face of the waters.' Lachem (bread, food) — in the context of the wisdom tradition, the image may also evoke grain sent out in commerce (grain-ships on the Nile or Mediterranean). Al penei hamayim (upon the face of the waters) — the investment cast into the uncertain medium of trade or generosity, where outcomes are not controlled. Ki berov hayamim timtzaenu: 'for after many days you will find it again.' Berov hayamim (in the abundance of days, after many days) — the return is real but temporally deferred. The wisdom is about the willingness to release what one has into uncertain channels, trusting the return that cannot be predicted or controlled.
2: ten cheleq leshivah vegam lishmonah: 'Give a portion to seven, even to eight.' The surplus-giving beyond calculation: seven is a round number suggesting fullness; vegam lishmonah (and even to eight) pushes past the complete number, indicating generosity that exceeds what would be strictly required. Ki lo teda mah yihyeh ra'ah al ha'aretz: 'for you do not know what calamity may come upon the land.' The rationale for surplus generosity is epistemic — future calamity is unknown, and having distributed generously creates the relational and social network that sustains people through calamity. Generosity as an investment strategy under uncertainty.
3: im yimmal'u he'avim geshem al ha'aretz yariqu: 'If the clouds are full of rain, they pour it on the earth.' The cloud-rain example is of determined outcomes: filled clouds pour — there is no uncertainty. Ve'im yipol etz badrom o batzafon: 'and if a tree falls to the south or to the north.' Bimqom sheyyipol haetz sham yihyu: 'in the place where the tree falls, there it lies.' The fallen tree lies where it fell — past events are fixed. Together Ecclesiastes 11:3-4 frame the dilemma of action under uncertainty: some things are determined (clouds pour, trees stay where they fall), and the person who waits for perfect conditions (no wind, no clouds) will never sow or reap. Past fixity and future uncertainty together call for present action.
4: shomer ruach lo yizra: 'Whoever watches the wind will not sow.' Ruach (wind, spirit) — the dual meaning will be activated in verse 5. Here it is wind: the farmer who watches wind conditions looking for the perfect windless day will never sow his seed. Vero'eh be'avim lo yiqtzor: 'and whoever watches the clouds will not reap.' The weather-watcher who waits for certainty before acting produces nothing. The proverb commends action in the face of imperfect conditions rather than the paralysis of perpetual uncertainty-management. The person who never acts until all risks are eliminated makes the worst decision by default.
5: ka'asher einekha yode'a mah derech haruach: 'Just as you do not know the path of the wind.' Ruach here carries both meanings simultaneously — wind (the meteorological phenomenon) and spirit/breath (the animating principle). Ke'atzamot bebeten hamele'ah: 'like the bones in the womb of a pregnant woman' — the formation of the skeletal structure in utero. Kakah lo teda et ma'aseh ha'elohim asher ya'aseh et hakol: 'so you do not know the work of God who makes everything.' The two unknowable things — the path of the wind-spirit and the formation of bones in the womb — are both examples of God's creational work that proceeds beyond human observation. Qohelet uses this observation to motivate action despite ignorance rather than paralysis.
6: bazzoqer zera et zar'ekha: 'In the morning sow your seed.' Zera... zar'ekha — the verb and noun from the same root for maximal emphasis on the act of sowing. Velaerev al tanach yadekha: 'and in the evening do not let your hand rest.' The double-shift work ethic: sow morning and evening without ceasing. Ki einekha yode'a ei zeh yikshur: 'for you do not know which will succeed.' The epistemic uncertainty (lo yeda) that would paralyze the weather-watcher instead motivates the double-sower — precisely because you don't know which planting will prosper, you maximize both and trust one or both to produce. The o shneihem ke'echad tovim (or whether both will be equally good) is the hoped-for outcome of comprehensive diligence.
7: umetuq ha'or: 'Light is sweet.' Metuq (sweet) — sensory pleasure applied to sight. Vetov la'einayim lir'ot et hashemesh: 'and good for the eyes to see the sun.' This two-word observation (umetuq ha'or) opens the joy-section of Ecclesiastes 11:7-12:1 — the positive commendation of present delight before the aging and death section of chapter 12. The sweetness of light is not an argument for hedonism but a genuine acknowledgment of the goodness of created existence. Qohelet's recurring commendation of joy (simcha) is grounded in the goodness of the created world even within the context of its transience.
8: ki im shanah harbe yichyeh ha'adam: 'If a person lives many years.' Bekhullam yismach: 'in all of them let him rejoice.' The comprehensive-joy instruction: joy through the full length of life, not reserved for certain seasons. Veyizkor et yemei hachoshekh ki harbeh yihyu: 'but let him remember the days of darkness, for they will be many.' Yemei hachoshekh (days of darkness) — approaching death, the long undifferentiated darkness before the chapter 12 imagery. Kol sheba havel: 'all that comes is vapor.' The joy-and-remember structure: joy is commended, but the memory of darkness prevents the joy from becoming blind cheerfulness. Both are real.
9: simach bachur beyaldutekha: 'Rejoice, young man, in your youth.' Beyaldutekha (in your childhood/youth) — the unrecoverable time of youthful energy. Vetov libekha bimei bechurotekha: 'let your heart be good in the days of your prime.' Bechurot (prime of youth, vigor of youth) — the fullness of young strength. Vehalekh bidarkhei libekha uvemara'ei einekha: 'and walk in the ways of your heart and the sights of your eyes.' Qohelet endorses the full use of the young person's capacities — and then adds the crucial frame: veda ki al kol eleh yeviaakha ha'elohim bamishpat: 'and know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment.' The joy is real and commended; the accounting is also real and unavoidable.
10: vehasir ka'as milibekha: 'Banish anxiety from your heart.' Ka'as (vexation, irritation, anxiety — the gnawing frustration Qohelet has observed throughout) — here commanded away. Veha'aver ra'ah mibbesarekha: 'and put away pain from your body.' Ki heyaldut vehashachachar havel: 'for childhood and the prime of youth are vapor.' Shachachar (dawn, the first light) — a rare form; the youth-dawn connection suggests the fleeting first light of the day as the image for youth: beautiful, brief, already passing as it arrives. The verse closes the youth-section with the hevel verdict on youth itself — the vapor-breath that Qohelet has used throughout finally lands on the most energetic stage of human life.
